title: Executive Brief
subtitle: Sutton SignWriting as research infrastructure
summary: A concise forwardable presentation that states the mission, support gap, and opportunity around Sutton SignWriting.
synopsis: A short executive framing of the support case. It begins with the project mission, then explains what Sutton SignWriting is, why its existing asset base matters, why Brazil now functions as the clearest documented empirical center, why South Korea now provides a current official institutional proof point, what support gap remains, and what a PI or host institution would unlock by giving the work a stable research home.

slides.title: Mission Statement
slides.summary: The mission is to give Sutton SignWriting a stable academic home so its existing standards, tools, data, and research pathways can be maintained, studied, preserved, and extended.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for executive brief overview.
bullets: Stabilize an existing global system
Support research, preservation, and growth
Move from informal stewardship to stable institutional support
slides.speech: The mission can be stated directly. Sutton SignWriting already exists as a globally used writing and technical ecosystem, but it still lacks a stable academic home. The goal is to support what already exists so that standards, tools, datasets, and research pathways can be maintained, studied, extended, and handed forward with continuity.
slides.notes: Open with the project purpose in one plain institutional sentence.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Existing global system] --> B[Durable academic home]
  B --> C[Stabilization]
  B --> D[Research]
  B --> E[Preservation]
  B --> F[Growth]

slides.title: What Sutton SignWriting Is
slides.summary: SignWriting is a structured visual script for signed languages, used internationally and supported by real tools, encodings, and data.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what Sutton SignWriting is.
bullets: Not reducible to sound and visually structured
Expressive enough for full signed languages
Used in education, research, and community contexts
Not a glossing or transcription layer on spoken language
slides.speech: Sutton SignWriting is a system for writing signed languages using a structured visual script. It is not reducible to sound, it is expressive enough for full signed languages, and it is already used internationally in education, research, and community settings. Unlike glossing or transcription systems, it is a direct written representation of signed language.
slides.notes: This is the definitional slide. Keep the distinction from glossing clear because it matters for academic readers.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Signed Language] --> B[Direct Written Representation]
  B --> C[Education]
  B --> D[Research]
  B --> E[Community Use]

slides.title: Existing Asset Base
slides.summary: The ecosystem already includes domains, tools, standards, code, and datasets under ongoing technical care.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for existing SignWriting asset base.
bullets: Long-running domains and public sites
Production tools such as SignPuddle and SignMaker
ISWA 2010, FSW, SWU, fonts, and libraries
Datasets, dictionaries, and written materials
slides.speech: The existing asset base is one of the strongest reasons to take this seriously. The ecosystem already includes public domains, production tools, formal encoding standards, fonts, software libraries, packages, and accumulated datasets and written materials. This is not a speculative or early-stage system. It is a mature distributed platform developed over more than two decades.
slides.notes: Say directly that this already exists.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Domains and Sites] --> F[Existing Asset Base]
  B[Tools and Applications] --> F
  C[Standards and Encodings] --> F
  D[Code and Libraries] --> F
  E[Datasets and Dictionaries] --> F

slides.title: The Current Gap
slides.summary: The main problem is not missing ideas or missing software, but missing stable coordinated support for work that already exists.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the current gap.
bullets: Distributed maintenance across individuals
Uneven documentation and onboarding
Limited institutional anchoring
Fragile continuity for long-term stewardship
slides.speech: What is missing is not the idea, the community, or the software. What is missing is stable coordinated support. At present the ecosystem depends on distributed maintenance, uneven documentation, and limited institutional anchoring. The gap is not absence of work. The gap is absence of stable support for work that is already active.
slides.notes: This is the key transition slide. Keep the contrast clean: not missing idea, missing support.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Existing Infrastructure] --> B[Limited Institutional Support]
  B --> C[Fragile Continuity]
  C --> D[Risk to Preservation, Tools, and Research Use]

slides.title: Why This Matters for Research
slides.summary: Infrastructure turns isolated efforts into scalable programs across corpora, standards, computation, and visual-language research.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for research value of infrastructure.
bullets: Corpora and reproducible datasets
Machine learning and computational linguistics
Visual language processing and literacy research
Cross-linguistic comparison and documentation
slides.speech: Treating SignWriting as shared research support changes what becomes possible. A stronger support structure would support corpus development, reproducible datasets, machine learning and computational linguistics, research into visual language processing and literacy, and cross-linguistic comparison and documentation. Brazil now gives this case a stronger empirical center, because real institutions, learners, and materials are already visible there. South Korea adds a current official public dictionary proof point. Without stronger support, these remain isolated efforts. With it, they become scalable research programs.
slides.notes: Move from stewardship to academic upside here. Use the phrase 'scalable research programs.'
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Stable Infrastructure] --> B[Corpora]
  A --> C[Reusable Datasets]
  A --> D[Computational Research]
  A --> E[Visual Language Studies]
  A --> F[Cross-Linguistic Documentation]

slides.title: Why Now
slides.summary: The timing is strong because interest in multimodal language work is growing while the SignWriting technical base and research path are now concrete enough to become a grant-ready program.
bullets: Growing multimodal and non-speech language interest
More emphasis on data sharing and reusable datasets
Maturing tooling around SignWriting
Visible but under-supported global user base
slides.speech: Several factors make this a timely opportunity. There is growing interest in multimodal and non-speech language processing, increased emphasis on data sharing and reusable datasets, maturing tooling around SignWriting, and a visible global user base that is still under-supported. The research path is also clearer than before: reading and literacy work, Brazil-grounded institutional and materials work, South Korea as a current institutional proof point, and infrastructure-plus-dataset development can now be described as one connected program. This creates a narrow window where institutional support can convert existing work into a stable, grant-ready program.
slides.notes: This is the timing slide. Keep it forward-looking and avoid sounding alarmist.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Research Interest] --> E[Why Now]
  B[Data Sharing Pressure] --> E
  C[Maturing Tooling] --> E
  D[Global User Base] --> E
  E --> F[Grant-Ready Infrastructure Opportunity]

slides.title: What a PI or Host Institution Would Unlock
slides.summary: A stable home would turn informal stewardship into a stronger institutional program through documentation, grants, partnerships, and coordinated development.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what a PI or host institution would unlock.
bullets: Coordinated development of tools and standards
Systematic documentation and onboarding
Grant proposals grounded in real assets
Partnerships across linguistics, education, CS, and neuroscience
Translation and access to especially important international works
slides.speech: A host institution would be building on an existing technical and data foundation, not initiating a new system. With a stable home, the project could transition from informal stewardship to a stronger institutional program. That would enable coordinated development, systematic documentation, grant preparation grounded in real assets, interdisciplinary partnerships, translation and access work around important international materials, and long-term preservation planning.
slides.notes: Make the practical upside concrete enough for a PI to imagine ownership.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[PI or Host Institution] --> B[Institutional Anchoring]
  A --> C[Research Framing]
  A --> D[Grant Development]
  A --> E[Program Integration]
  B --> F[Formal Infrastructure]

slides.title: Closing
slides.summary: Sutton SignWriting already works as a globally used system; with the right home, it becomes a resource others can rely on and fund with confidence.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for executive brief closing.
bullets: Working global system
Software, data, and standards already exist
Stable institutional support is the missing layer
slides.speech: The most compelling framing is simple. Sutton SignWriting already exists as a working, globally used system with software, data, and standards. What it lacks is stable institutional support. With the right home, it becomes a resource that others can rely on, build on, and fund with confidence.
slides.notes: End with the simplest version of the case so it feels easy to forward after hearing.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Working Global System] --> B[Durable Institutional Support]
  B --> C[Infrastructure Others Can Rely On]
  C --> D[Research, Funding, and Growth]
